Notice Of Motion And Motion To Amend Complaint To Add Punitive Damages; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Jul-01-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (all through Court Call):
Nicholas Carlin Esq.; Brian Conlon Esq. 415-398-0900 of Phillips Erlewine iven & Carlin LLP for PLaintiff Jennifer Cooper.
Emily Fowler Esq. 415-398-0200 of Vogl Meredith Burke LLP for Defendant Emerald 100 Van Ness.
Jodie Steinberg Esq. 714-988-0023 x704 of Law Offices of Jennings & Legaspi for Defendat/Cross-Complainant Emerald Fund.
Sumari Sandhu Esq. 916-634-7800 of Sandhu Law Group for Defendant Hi Neighbor LLC and 100 Van Ness LLC.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF JENNIFER COOPER's Motion To Amend Complaint To Add Punitive Damages.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Jennifer Cooper's motion to amend complaint to add punitive damages prayer and allegations is granted. In its prior order, the court struck the punitive damages allegations on the ground that they did not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others. Plaintiff now adduces additional facts that she contends establish that defendants were on notice for several years of dangerous conditions on its property, were aware that those conditions had caused numerous injuries, and were also aware of potential treatments that would have reduced the risk but failed timely to adopt them. The Court cannot conclude as a matter of law at this stage of the proceedings that defendants' alleged conduct could not support an award of punitive damages, which generally is an issue to be resolved on a factual record, either by summary adjudication or by a jury. (Cf. Johnson & Johnson Talcum Powder Cases (2019) 37 Cal.App.5th 292, 332-335 [trial court properly granted JNOV for defendant as to punitive damages because "no reasonable jury could find [plaintiff's] evidence provided clear and convincing proof of malice"]; Butte Fire Cases (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1168-1176 [trial court properly granted summary adjudication for defendant on issue of malice, where defendant presented evidence that "negated any inference that the company acted despicably, and with willful and conscious disregard for the rights and safety of others," and plaintiffs did not present sufficient evidence to raise a triable issue of fact as to malice]; Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1213 [issue resolved on summary adjudication].)

Plaintiff's Counsel Nicholas Carlin to re-submit a revised order which adds language re: a supplemental responsive pleading that all parties agree to. If parties are unable to agree on a revised proposed order, the Court will sign the proposed order that was submitted by email. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
